content:
RxJS vs Signals

3 min read
8 hours ago

--

| Feature | RxJS | Signals |
| ------------------------ | --------- | ---------- |
| Current Value | ❌ | ✅ |
| Auto Dependency Tracking | ❌ | ✅ |
| Fine-Grained Updates | ❌ | ✅ |
| Subscriptions Required | ✅ | ❌ |
| Async Streams | ✅ | ⚠️ Limited |
| Learning Curve | High | Low |
| Zone-less Support | Limited | Excellent |
| UI State | Good | Excellent |
| Event Streams/WebSockets | Excellent | Use RxJS |Before Angular Signals, state management relied heavily on:
RxJS Observables
↓
Subscriptions
↓
Change Detection
↓
UI UpdatesSignals introduce a more direct and efficient reactive model.
1. Fine-Grained Reactivity
Traditional RxJS
Suppose your state is:
interface AppState {
users: User[];
orders: Order[];
products: Product[];
}When state changes:
store.select(selectUsers)Angular often reevaluates selectors and component trees.
Signals
const users = signal<User[]>([]);
const orders = signal<Order[]>([]);When:
users.set(newUsers);Only consumers of users() update.
users changed
↓
users consumers update
↓
nothing else updatesThis is called fine-grained reactivity.
2. No Manual Subscriptions
RxJS
this.userService.users$
.subscribe(users => {
this.users = users;
});Need:
takeUntil()
destroy$
unsubscribe()Potential memory leaks.
Signals
readonly users = signal<User[]>([]);Template:
@for(user of users(); track user.id) {
...
}No:
subscribe()
unsubscribe()
takeUntil()required.
3. Better Performance
Observable Flow
Observable emits
↓
Async Pipe
↓
Zone.js
↓
Change Detection
↓
Component Tree CheckSignal Flow
Signal changes
↓
Direct dependency tracking
↓
Affected UI updatesMuch less work.
Example
Imagine:
Dashboard
├── Users Widget
├── Orders Widget
├── Revenue Widget
├── Notifications WidgetChanging:
orders.set(...)Only:
Orders Widgetrerenders.
Not the entire dashboard.
4. Computed State is Simpler
RxJS
totalUsers$ = combineLatest([
users$,
admins$
]).pipe(
map(([users, admins]) => users.length + admins.length)
);Signals
const totalUsers = computed(
() => users().length + admins().length
);Much easier to read.
5. Automatic Dependency Tracking
You don’t specify dependencies.
const fullName = computed(
() => firstName() + ' ' + lastName()
);Angular automatically tracks:
firstName
lastNameNo manual wiring.
6. Zone-less Ready
Angular’s future is moving toward:
Zone.js ❌
Signals ✅Traditional Angular:
User Action
↓
Zone.js intercepts
↓
Global Change DetectionSignals:
Signal changed
↓
Known dependencies updateNo Zone.js needed.
This is one reason Angular 19/20 heavily promotes Signals.
7. Less Boilerplate than NgRx Store
Traditional NgRx:
Action
Reducer
Selector
Effect
FacadeExample feature:
users.actions.ts
users.reducer.ts
users.selectors.ts
users.effects.ts
users.facade.tsSignal Store:
export const UserStore = signalStore(
withState({
users: []
}),
withMethods((store) => ({
loadUsers() {}
}))
);One store.
Less code.
8. Easier Mental Model
Observable:
Stream of values over timeSignal:
Current value that can changeExample:
count()feels like:
countbut reactive.
Developers generally find Signals easier to learn than advanced RxJS operators.
9. Perfect for UI State
Things like:
Loading State
Selected Tab
Theme
Sidebar Open
Filters
Pagination
Search Text
Form Draftbecome very simple.
const loading = signal(false);
loading.set(true);instead of:
loadingSubject.next(true);The Angular 20 Architecture Trend
Modern Angular applications are increasingly using:
Backend API
↓
HttpClient
↓
RxJS (async operations)
↓
Signal Store
↓
Signals
↓
Angular TemplatesRule of Thumb
Use RxJS for async workflows:

• HTTP

• WebSockets

• Event streams

• Retry logic

• Polling
Use Signals for application state:

• User data

• Dashboard state

• Filters

• UI state

• Cached API data
This combination gives the best performance and developer experience in Angular 19/20.
